explicit_text: I was born and raised on my father's plantation in Newberry County, South Carolina, on May 17, 1884. My childhood and early youth were spent there, attending the old field public school in the autumn and winter and working on the farm in the spring and summer. Farming was tough with uncertain crops, and in 1898 we faced a particularly harsh year with storms and drought that devastated our crops. It was in Newberry County that my family decided to leave the farm and seek a more stable livelihood in the city.
implicit_text: I was born and raised on my father's plantation in a rural county in South Carolina, on May 17, 1884. My childhood and early youth were spent there, attending the old field public school in the autumn and winter and working on the farm in the spring and summer. Farming was tough with uncertain crops, and in 1898 we faced a particularly harsh year with storms and drought that devastated our crops. It was in that county that my family decided to leave the farm and seek a more stable livelihood in the city.

explicit_text: In January 1899, my family moved to Columbia, South Carolina, after selling our farm in Newberry County. We searched for a house and a building to open a grocery store, and soon began living the life of a big town. Columbia was a place of keen competition, but my father’s grocery store prospered on a cash-only basis. I also worked in Columbia at the Stork greenhouses starting in 1904 and later began my florist business there, initially in Thomas Drug Store and then expanding to other locations in the city.
implicit_text: In January 1899, my family moved to the capital city of South Carolina after selling our farm in the rural county. We searched for a house and a building to open a grocery store, and soon began living the life of a big town. This city was a place of keen competition, but my father’s grocery store prospered on a cash-only basis. I also worked there at the Stork greenhouses starting in 1904 and later began my florist business, initially in a local drug store and then expanding to other locations in the city.

explicit_text: Lexington, South Carolina, was the place where my father closed the sale of our farm in Newberry County. After negotiations with a timber investor, the deal was finalized at the courthouse in Lexington in late 1898 or early 1899. This sale allowed our family to move to Columbia and start a new life away from the uncertainties of farming.
implicit_text: A town in South Carolina was where my father closed the sale of our farm in the rural county. After negotiations with a timber investor, the deal was finalized at the courthouse in that town in late 1898 or early 1899. This sale allowed our family to move to the state capital and start a new life away from the uncertainties of farming.

explicit_text: My oldest brother, Toole, became the manager of the Studebaker Automobile Agency in Columbia. While I was building my florist business, he was doing well in the automobile industry. It’s interesting to see how our family members found different paths to success in Columbia.
implicit_text: My oldest brother, Toole, became the manager of a well-known automobile agency in the state capital. While I was building my florist business, he was doing well in the automobile industry. It’s interesting to see how our family members found different paths to success in that city.

explicit_text: My youngest brother, J. E. Sligh, works as an engineer for the South Carolina Highway Department. While I was focused on my florist business, he pursued a career in public service related to transportation infrastructure. It’s a point of pride that my family members have established themselves in various respectable professions.

It’s a point of pride that my family members have established themselves in various respectable professions.

explicit_text: One of my sisters is married and lives in Hendersonville, North Carolina. She is Mrs. H. C. Tate, and although I am based in Columbia, I have family ties extending to Hendersonville. It’s nice to have relatives in different places, showing how our family has spread out over the years.

Tate, and although I am based in the South Carolina capital, I have family ties extending to that North Carolina town. explicit_text: I began working at Stork Greenhouses in Columbia in 1904, earning fifty cents a day at first. My duties included maintaining proper heat and moisture for the flowers, and I spent eight years studying and experimenting there to learn the florist trade. Though the work was physically demanding, it laid the foundation for my later success in owning my own florist business.

My duties included maintaining proper heat and moisture for the flowers, and I spent eight years studying and experimenting there to learn the florist trade. Though the work was physically demanding, it laid the foundation for my later success in owning my own florist business.

explicit_text: In 1912, I started my florist business in a small cubbyhole space inside Thomas Drug Store on Main Street in Columbia. I paid three dollars a week in rent and began with a loan of twenty dollars from a friend. The business grew quickly, and I was soon able to pay back the loan and expand beyond that initial space.
implicit_text: In 1912, I started my florist business in a small cubbyhole space inside a well-known drug store on Main Street in the state capital. I paid three dollars a week in rent and began with a loan of twenty dollars from a friend. The business grew quickly, and I was soon able to pay back the loan and expand beyond that initial space.

explicit_text: As my florist business grew, I moved from Thomas Drug Store to a larger six-dollar-a-week space at Miot's Drug Store at 1430 Main Street. This move allowed me to accommodate more customers and expand my operations before eventually leasing quarters in the Imperial Hotel. Miot's was an important step in the growth of my business.

explicit_text: Eventually, I leased space in the Imperial Hotel in Columbia, where I continue to run my florist business. The hotel location brought regular orders from hotels, hospitals, funerals, and weddings, especially during busy times like the mobilization at Camp Jackson in 1917. The Imperial Hotel became the central hub for my flourishing florist trade.

explicit_text: In 1917, when troops began mobilizing at Camp Jackson near Columbia, the demand for flowers from my florist business increased tremendously. By spring 1918, with 40,000 to 50,000 men and officers training there, the influenza epidemic was taking a heavy toll, and florists like me were working hard to meet the high demand for funeral and hospital flowers. Camp Jackson was a significant event that affected my business during those years.

explicit_text: The 1918 influenza pandemic hit Columbia hard while Camp Jackson was full of troops, causing many deaths. This tragic event led to an unprecedented demand for flowers for funerals and hospitals, which kept my florist business very busy. Despite the sorrow, the pandemic underscored how certain businesses like florists remain steady even in tough times.
